pairs.company_name: OHSU (AKA Oregon Health and Science University)

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about ohsu's revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hi Tammy,

Read your post about Baker Tilly joining as OHSU's 2026/2027 Gold Sponsor. Investing in advisory partnerships signals you're actively working the financial recovery problem.

OHSU's $110 million operating loss in FY2024 puts real pressure on every dollar in professional revenue. When expenses outpace reimbursement, the natural reflex is cost-cutting, but often the faster lever is recapturing revenue already earned.

We've seen academic health centers in similar positions recover 8-12% of net revenue by closing coding accuracy gaps, without adding headcount.

Curious whether OHSU is prioritizing denial prevention or back-end recovery right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought about ohsu's professional billing accuracy
emails.email_body:
Hey Tammy,

Academic medical centers running complex, multi-specialty volume are uniquely exposed to coding undercapture. Physician documentation complexity and coder workload create gaps that compound quietly over months.

We recently worked with a comparable academic health system that identified $6.2M in annual undercapture across their professional fee billing, found through a focused coding accuracy audit. The fix required no new hires.

With OHSU's S&P rating under pressure, precision on the revenue side matters more than ever.

Is closing professional billing accuracy gaps something your team is actively working on this year?

emails.subject_line: before we close the loop, tammy
emails.email_body:
Hello Tammy,

One last thought given what OHSU is navigating. Several revenue cycle directors at peer academic medical centers have told us the single fastest financial win they found was autonomous coding review, not a lengthy implementation, just running their actual claims through and seeing where money was left on the table.

We can run a no-commitment analysis on a sample of OHSU's professional claims and show you exactly what that looks like in your environment.

Would it help to see how similar academic health centers approached this?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about ohsu's revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hi Tammy,

Read your post recognizing Baker Tilly as OHSU's 2026/2027 Gold Sponsor. Engaging advisory partners at that level tells us you're building the infrastructure for a serious financial turnaround.

OHSU reported a $110 million operating loss in FY2024 on a $4.9 billion budget, following a $44 million loss the prior year. S&P downgraded the system's financial health rating as a result. When inflation-driven expenses and labor costs outpace revenue growth, professional revenue cycle accuracy becomes one of the few controllable levers left.

Academic health centers with comparable complexity typically see 6-10% undercapture in physician fee coding. That translates to $15-25M on a budget OHSU's size.

Curious whether OHSU is prioritizing denial prevention or recapturing undercoded professional claims right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought about ohsu's professional billing performance
emails.email_body:
Hey Tammy,

Academic medical centers face a distinct coding challenge. Multi-specialty volume, complex clinical documentation, and coder-to-provider ratios that rarely keep up with growth create systematic undercapture that manual audits miss at scale.

We partnered with a large academic health system last year, similar size and complexity to OHSU, and surfaced $6.2M in annual undercaptured professional fee revenue across their employed physician groups. The analysis ran against their live claims data in under three weeks, required no IT integration, and flagged specific CPT and modifier patterns by specialty.

For a system managing through an S&P downgrade, that kind of precision on the revenue side is worth quantifying.

Is identifying and closing professional coding gaps something your team has capacity to pursue this fiscal year?

emails.subject_line: last note, tammy, on ohsu's revenue recovery path
emails.email_body:
Hello Tammy,

I want to be direct given the financial context at OHSU. Systems in active recovery mode typically have two paths: cut costs further or recover revenue more precisely. Cost-cutting has real limits, especially with labor union agreements and Oregon's staffing law constraints.

The revenue precision path is faster and doesn't require headcount. Several revenue cycle directors at peer academic systems ran a sample analysis against their actual claims, no commitment, no IT project, and discovered 8-12% undercapture they weren't aware of.

We can do the same for OHSU's professional billing in two to three weeks, using your actual claims.

Would it help to see how comparable academic health centers quantified this and what they did next?

Precision on the revenue side, particularly professional billing accuracy, is often the fastest lever available without adding staff.

Open to a 15-minute call this week?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about chop's revenue integrity approach
emails.email_body:
Hi Kim,

Read your post about the One Big Beautiful Bill Act and the pressure it puts on safety-net providers. Your point about providers needing to build financial resilience before policy headwinds hit landed clearly.

CHOP's revenue integrity function sits at the intersection of HIM and clinical documentation. When policy shifts compress reimbursement, coding accuracy gaps that were tolerable become material.

We work with pediatric health systems to close those gaps autonomously, without adding coding staff.

Curious whether CHOP is prioritizing denial prevention or upstream coding accuracy right now?

P.S. - We offer a free 30-day pilot with your actual claims. We are HITRUST, SOC II, and HIPAA Compliant.

emails.subject_line: a thought about chop's coding accuracy exposure
emails.email_body:
Hey Kim,

Your background spanning revenue integrity and HIM is exactly the vantage point where undercapture becomes visible. Pediatric coding is inherently complex, higher acuity cases, complex modifiers, subspecialty documentation variability.

We ran an analysis for a comparable children's hospital and surfaced $4.1M in annual undercaptured revenue from coding gaps their internal audits had missed.

Is closing coding accuracy gaps across CHOP's physician and facility billing something your team is actively prioritizing?

P.S. - We are HITRUST, SOC II, and HIPAA Compliant.

pairs.company_name: WVU Medicine (AKA West Virginia University Health System)

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about wvu medicine's expansion trajectory
emails.email_body:
Hi Carrie,

Read your post about the talent in healthcare and how often it goes unrecognized. That lens shapes how strong operators approach growth, by building systems that don't depend on any single person.

WVU Medicine absorbing Independence Health System, five hospitals, $800M commitment, Epic implementation, is one of the most complex integration plays in the region right now.

Revenue cycle integrity across newly acquired facilities is the variable that most systems underestimate in year one.

Curious whether WVU Medicine is prioritizing coding standardization or denial management as you bring Independence into the fold?

emails.subject_line: a thought about wvu medicine's revenue cycle as you scale
emails.email_body:
Hey Carrie,

System integrations at WVU Medicine's scale, 25 hospitals becoming 30, carry a predictable revenue cycle risk: acquired facilities code differently, and the gap compounds until someone measures it.

We've worked with health systems mid-integration to baseline coding accuracy across legacy and acquired facilities before full Epic go-live. The findings typically surface 6-10% revenue undercapture in acquired sites.

Is coding accuracy standardization across the Independence Health System acquisition something your team is actively planning for?

emails.subject_line: last note before the integration closes, carrie
emails.email_body:
Hello Carrie,

The window between acquisition announcement and Epic go-live is when revenue cycle gaps are easiest to find and fix. After go-live, they get buried in transition noise.

WVU Medicine's $3 billion capital investment over the next decade requires the revenue foundation to hold. We've helped comparable health systems entering major expansions run a rapid coding accuracy baseline across new facilities, no IT integration required, results in weeks.

Would it help to see how similar health systems approached this before their integration milestones?

Hi Tammy,

Read your post about Baker Tilly joining as OHSU's 2026/2027 Gold Sponsor. External advisory investment during a financial recovery signals you're approaching this with the right seriousness.

OHSU's $110M operating loss in FY2024 followed a $44M loss the prior year. When losses compound at that rate, the pressure on professional revenue cycle to perform at maximum accuracy becomes acute.

Curious whether OHSU is prioritizing denial prevention or recapturing undercoded professional claims right now?

emails.subject_line: a thought about your role in ohsu's revenue recovery
emails.email_body:
Hey Tammy,

As Senior Director of Professional Revenue Cycle, you're holding one of the most critical functions in OHSU's financial recovery. Professional fee billing accuracy at a multi-specialty academic health center is where the margin difference between recovery and continued loss often lives.

The directors in your position who move fastest on this typically start by running their actual claims through an automated review, to quantify the gap before committing to a fix.

Is running that kind of diagnostic analysis something your team has bandwidth for right now?

emails.subject_line: what revenue cycle directors at peer academic health centers are doing
emails.email_body:
Hello Tammy,

Revenue cycle directors at academic health centers managing through operating losses are consistently finding the same thing: coding undercapture in professional fee billing is larger than internal audits suggest, and it's fixable without adding headcount.

The ones who've acted on it recovered 8-12% of net professional revenue within two quarters.

Would it help to see how peer academic health centers approached this and what they found?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about UW Medicine's payer mix
emails.email_body:
Hi Jeremy,

Noticed UW Medicine recently lost in-network status with Regence BlueShield, Humana, PacificSource, and Wellpoint Medicare Advantage plans for 2026. Four payers in one cycle is significant exposure for lab and pathology revenue.

When payer mix shifts this fast, coding accuracy on the remaining volume becomes the margin lever that matters most.

Orgs that tightened coding operations ahead of similar payer disruptions recovered 15-20% of at-risk revenue within two quarters.

Curious whether UW Medicine is prioritizing coding optimization or payer contract renegotiation first?

emails.subject_line: a thought about UW Medicine's lab revenue exposure
emails.email_body:
Hey Jeremy,

With federal funding under pressure and four payer contracts gone, lab and pathology is carrying more revenue risk than it was 12 months ago.

We work with academic medical centers in similar positions. One recovered $4.2M in annual lab revenue by closing coding gaps on high-complexity pathology cases alone.

Happy to share the breakdown of where that recovery came from.

Is coding accuracy on complex lab cases something your team is actively working on right now?

emails.subject_line: last note on UW Medicine's revenue cycle
emails.email_body:
Hello Jeremy,

UW Medicine is managing federal funding cuts, four fewer MA payers, and a 33,000-person system's worth of operational complexity.

We have helped revenue directors at comparable academic health systems find 12-18% more in captured revenue without adding headcount.

We only have capacity for one new academic medical center partnership this quarter.

Would it help to see how similar orgs approached this given the payer mix shift you are facing?

emails.subject_line: something we noticed about Johns Hopkins's coding operations
emails.email_body:
Hi April,

Read your post about speaking to an international audience on healthcare operations. That visibility at the executive level shapes what gets prioritized internally.

At RapidClaims, we work with COOs at health systems like Johns Hopkins where coding throughput is a hidden margin lever. RapidClaims autonomous coding processes claims at 10x speed with 98%+ accuracy.

Systems that deployed it recovered 14% more revenue in the first quarter without adding staff.

Curious whether Johns Hopkins is prioritizing coding throughput or denial reduction as the bigger revenue opportunity?

emails.subject_line: a thought about Johns Hopkins's revenue cycle efficiency
emails.email_body:
Hey April,

Johns Hopkins operates at a scale where even a 1% coding accuracy improvement moves millions of dollars.

RapidClaims has worked with academic medical centers of similar complexity. One COO-level initiative targeting autonomous coding for high-acuity cases generated $6.8M in recovered revenue in year one.

Happy to share the operational model behind that result.

Is autonomous coding for complex cases something your team is actively evaluating right now?
